summary: Repeated Surface.Snapshot() calls in OnPaintSurface on a large Blazor WASM canvas (8000x2000) without disposing previous SKImage instances exhausts WASM heap memory after ~34 iterations, crashing in SkData::MakeWithCopy during pixel allocation.

stepsToReproduce: Create a Blazor WASM app with SKCanvasView sized 8000x2000
In OnPaintSurface, call e.Surface.Snapshot() on every paint cycle and store in a field without disposing the previous image
Trigger repeated paints via mouse move (Invalidate on each mouse move)
After ~34 iterations, crash occurs in native memory allocation

bugSignals.errorMessage: Uncaught Error at operator new → SkData::PrivateNewWithCopy → MakeRasterCopyPriv → SkSurface_Raster::onNewImageSnapshot

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: The Snapshot() behavior (full pixel copy via MakeRasterCopyPriv) is unchanged across versions. The root cause is a usage pattern, not a version-specific defect.

analysis.summary: The reporter calls Surface.Snapshot() on every OnPaintSurface invocation on a large 8000x2000 canvas, storing the result in a field (layerBackGround) without disposing the previous SKImage. Each snapshot copies all pixel data (~64MB for 8000x2000x4 RGBA). After ~34 snapshots, the WASM heap (~2GB limit) is exhausted, causing an allocation failure in SkData::MakeWithCopy. This is a memory management issue in user code, not a SkiaSharp defect.
analysis.rationale: Classified as type/bug because the reporter experiences a crash, but the root cause is missing disposal of SKImage objects — a common memory management pattern that is correct-but-easy-to-misuse. The stack trace confirms OOM in native pixel allocation (operator new → SkData::PrivateNewWithCopy), not a logic error in SkiaSharp. Maintainer mattleibow confirmed the identical pattern in #576 (surface.Snapshot() stored without disposing old images causes memory growth). Suggesting close-as-by-design with a workaround.

keySignals.text: layerBackGround = e.Surface.Snapshot(); // Here the exception is raised
keySignals.interpretation: The old layerBackGround SKImage is overwritten without Dispose(), leaking ~64MB per iteration.

keySignals.text: When the canvas is smaller (300x400) the exception is not occurring
keySignals.interpretation: Smaller canvas = smaller snapshots (~480KB each), takes much longer to exhaust WASM heap. Confirms memory exhaustion as root cause.

keySignals.text: Restore Snapshot Number Restore 34 → Uncaught Error
keySignals.interpretation: 34 iterations × 64MB = ~2.2GB, exceeding typical WASM memory limit.
Crash in memory allocation during pixel copy — out-of-memory in WASM heap.

codeInvestigation.finding: Snapshot() calls sk_surface_new_image_snapshot which returns a new SKImage wrapping a full raster copy of all surface pixels. Each call allocates width×height×4 bytes of unmanaged memory.

SKImage implements ISKReferenceCounted and must be explicitly disposed. When not disposed, the underlying SkImage (and its pixel data) persists until GC finalizer runs — which may not happen fast enough on WASM to prevent OOM.

workarounds: Dispose the previous SKImage before creating a new snapshot: layerBackGround?.Dispose(); layerBackGround = e.Surface.Snapshot();
For drag-and-drop rendering, consider using SKPicture (via SKPictureRecorder) instead of pixel snapshots — it records draw commands rather than copying pixels, using far less memory.
nextQuestions: Would a SaveLayer/RestoreToCount approach work better for the reporter's drag-and-drop use case?
Should SkiaSharp log a warning when WASM heap usage is high during Snapshot()?

resolution.hypothesis: The reporter's code leaks SKImage instances by reassigning layerBackGround without disposing the old value. On WASM with a large canvas (8000x2000 = ~64MB per snapshot), this exhausts the heap after ~34 iterations.

proposals.title: Dispose old snapshot before creating new one
proposals.description: Add explicit disposal of the previous SKImage before reassigning. This is the minimal fix for the immediate crash.

proposals.title: Use SKPictureRecorder instead of pixel snapshots
proposals.description: Record draw commands into an SKPicture instead of copying pixels. SKPicture stores vector commands (small) rather than a full pixel buffer (64MB). Better suited for the drag-and-drop use case described.

resolution.recommendedProposal: Dispose old snapshot before creating new one
resolution.recommendedReason: Trivial one-line fix that directly addresses the memory leak. Matches the guidance from mattleibow on #576 for the identical pattern.

actionability.reason: The crash is caused by user code not disposing SKImage objects. SkiaSharp's Snapshot() correctly allocates pixel memory — the user must manage the lifecycle. Identical pattern confirmed by maintainer in #576.

actions.description: Apply bug, blazor, and WASM labels
Post workaround explaining the disposal pattern and alternative approach
Cross-reference #576 which has the identical Snapshot() memory pattern
Close as by-design — disposal is the caller's responsibility
